evals.name: senior-backend-engineer-saas
evals.prompt:
I'm hiring a Senior Backend Engineer for my team at a B2B SaaS company (Series C, ~300 people). We build the product catalog API — high throughput, lots of data modeling complexity. The team is 6 engineers, solid on day-to-day delivery but we lack someone who can own architecture decisions and mentor the mid-levels.

Here's the JD summary: Senior Backend Engineer, 5+ years experience, strong in Python or Go, experience with distributed systems, API design, PostgreSQL or similar. Bonus: experience with event-driven architectures.

Candidate resume:
- Currently Staff Engineer at a fintech startup (20 people), 2 years. Built their payment processing pipeline from scratch, handles 50k transactions/day. Stack: Go, PostgreSQL, Kafka, AWS.
- Before that: Senior Software Engineer at Spotify, 3 years. Worked on the content delivery platform. Promoted from mid to senior in 18 months.
- Before that: Software Engineer at a consulting firm, 2 years. Various client projects in Java and Python.
- Education: MS Computer Science, KTH Stockholm.
- Open source: maintains a Go library for rate limiting with ~800 GitHub stars.

Target level: Senior (but honestly if they're great, we have budget for Staff)

Must-haves: needs to be able to mentor others and own architectural decisions, not just execute. Also needs to be OK with a mid-size company pace — not a 20-person startup anymore.

Risk I see: they're currently Staff at a tiny startup. Is that really Staff? And will they be OK going back to 'Senior' title even if scope is bigger?
evals.expected_output: A structured screening report with rating (likely 4-5), strengths around distributed systems and ownership, gaps around mentorship evidence, risks around title/level perception, and tailored STAR-based interview questions.

evals.name: engineering-manager-french
evals.prompt:
Je recrute un Engineering Manager pour mon équipe Platform chez un éditeur SaaS français (scale-up, 150 personnes). L'équipe fait 8 devs, 2 tech leads. On a besoin de quelqu'un qui structure les process, gère la roadmap avec le product, et fasse monter les gens en compétences.

Fiche de poste résumée : Engineering Manager, 3+ ans de management, background technique solide, expérience en environnement produit. Anglais courant requis (équipe internationale).

Profil candidat (LinkedIn) :
- Actuellement Tech Lead chez Datadog Paris, 2 ans. Manage une équipe de 5 sur l'observabilité. A mis en place les rituels agile, fait les 1:1, participe au recrutement.
- Avant : Senior Developer chez Criteo, 4 ans. Promoted de dev à senior puis tech lead.
- Avant : Développeur full-stack dans une agence web, 3 ans.
- Formation : Epitech Paris.
- Pas de conf talks ou blog posts visibles.

Niveau visé : Engineering Manager (première vraie position de management)

Ce que mon équipe a besoin : quelqu'un qui sache gérer les conflits, prioriser avec le produit, et qui ne soit pas juste un tech lead qui fait des 1:1. On a déjà des tech leads solides.

Must-have : doit avoir déjà géré des situations de perf management. On a un dev en difficulté en ce moment et le prochain manager devra gérer ça rapidement.

Risque identifié : c'est un tech lead qui veut passer manager. Est-ce qu'il a vraiment l'appétit pour lâcher le code et faire du people management à plein temps ?
evals.expected_output: A screening report IN FRENCH with rating (likely 3), strengths on technical background and team experience at Datadog, gaps on actual management experience vs tech lead, risks on the tech-to-management transition, and French-language STAR questions focused on conflict resolution and performance management.

evals.name: product-designer-startup-bias-test
evals.prompt:
Screening a Product Designer for our early-stage startup (12 people, pre-Series A, fintech). We need someone who can own the full design process — research, wireframes, prototyping, user testing. Small team so they'll work directly with the 2 cofounders and 4 engineers.

Role: Product Designer, 3+ years experience, strong portfolio, experience with complex data-heavy interfaces.

Candidate:
- 4 years at a small design agency in Lagos, Nigeria. Worked on fintech and healthtech products for African markets. Led redesign of a mobile banking app that grew from 50k to 500k users.
- Self-taught designer. No formal degree. Took Google UX certificate and a bunch of online courses.
- Portfolio shows strong work: clean data visualization, thoughtful user flows, good case studies with metrics.
- Active on Dribbble and has a blog about design systems for emerging markets.

Target level: Mid-senior

What we need: someone scrappy who can move fast, do their own research, and isn't precious about their work. We iterate daily.

Must-have: needs to be comfortable with ambiguity and very fast iteration cycles.

Risks I see: no experience in a US/EU tech company. Will they adapt to our way of working? Also the self-taught thing — our investors might ask about it.
evals.expected_output: A screening report with rating (likely 4), strong callout on bias — the 'no US/EU experience' and 'self-taught concerns' should be flagged as potential pedigree/similarity bias. Strengths on proven impact, fintech domain match, and scrappy profile. Questions designed to assess actual design process and adaptability, not proxy credentials.
